description: The full civilian ownership form: broad dominion, exclusion, alienation, duration, and descent across the major codified systems.
notes: The nearest civilian anchor to the common-law fee simple absolute, but PRC 所有权 over land is structurally different: all urban land is state-owned (Art. 249) and all rural land is collectively owned (Art. 260). Individuals can only own buildings and structures, never the land beneath them. Slider ranges here reflect the DE/JP/TW concept of private ownership; PRC land ownership is a state prerogative with no private equivalent.

description: A long-duration building or superficies right carved out of ownership: strongly usable and transferable, but still term-bound rather than fully perpetual.
notes: Structurally a named real right, not a contractual lease. PRC 建设用地使用权 has mandatory maximum terms by use type: residential 70yr, commercial 40yr, industrial 50yr (国务院令第55号 Art. 12). Art. 359 provides for automatic renewal of residential terms, but conditions remain unsettled. Duration and inheritability ranges here span the cross-jurisdictional spread; PRC sits at the lower end.

description: A German usufruct right (Nießbrauch) focused on use and fruits rather than alienable ownership. No direct equivalent exists in JP, TW, or PRC law; 用益物権/用益物权 is a category name, not a holdable right.
notes: Common law tends to split this profile across life estates, trusts, and occupation rights rather than maintain a single canonical usufruct category.

description: A land-serving real right comparable to a servitude or easement: narrow in possession, enduring in duration, and usually tied to another parcel’s use.
notes: This is one of the cleanest cross-system mappings because both traditions accept a durable, non-possessory burden for limited use.

description: An accessory mortgage right geared toward secured value, not ordinary occupation or use: weak in possession, but durable enough to back credit and enforcement.

land._note: PRC mortgages attach to the use right (建设用地使用权), not ownership.

notes: The civilian mortgage remains closer to an accessory real security than the layered equitable devices familiar to common-law lawyers.

description: A pledge-style security right with a stronger possessory flavor than a mortgage, but still focused on collateral value rather than beneficial use.
notes: The match with common-law mortgage doctrine is partial at best because possession and accessory structure do more work here.

description: A historically distinctive Taiwanese right combining possession, use, and redemption-oriented structure in a way that does not stabilize into a familiar common-law estate.
notes: Useful precisely because it exposes where the common-law estate menu runs out of clean analogues.

description: A habitation right centered on residence rather than exchange: meaningful occupation and use, but little income, transfer, or descent.
notes: Common law tends to fragment this space into licenses, occupation rights, and specialized statutory protections rather than one named real right.

description: The foundational PRC rural land use right: households contract collectively owned land for agriculture and hold long-term possession, use, and income rights. The 2018 Three Rights Separation reform split this into 承包权 (contract right, non-transferable outside household) and 经营权 (management right, transferable and mortgageable).
notes: Approximately 300 million rural households hold this right. Term limits vary by land type: 耕地 30yr, 草地 30–50yr, 林地 30–70yr (Art. 332). The 经营权 is now separately transferable, exchangeable, and mortgageable (Art. 339–342), but the underlying 承包权 remains with the household. No clean common-law or other civil-law analogue exists.

description: A PRC-specific rural residential land use right: one household per homestead allocation from the village collective, with strong possession and use for housing but near-zero alienation outside the collective.
notes: Governed by Art. 362–365 and the Land Administration Law. Each household may hold only one homestead plot (一户一宅). Transfer outside the collective is heavily restricted in practice. Duration has no statutory ceiling but the right is tied to household membership in the collective. Inheritability is contested: current policy allows house inheritance (房地一体) but the underlying land right may revert when no eligible household members remain.

description: A possessory security interest: the creditor retains possession of the debtor's movable property until the secured debt is satisfied, with enforcement through auction or sale upon default.
notes: The third type of security interest in PRC law alongside 抵押权 and 质权. Unlike pledge, lien arises by operation of law rather than by agreement. The holder must not use the retained property (Art. 451 PRC) but may collect fruits to offset the debt. German law has no exact in rem equivalent; Zurückbehaltungsrecht (§ 273 BGB) is an obligational defense, not a property right.

description: A Taiwanese usufructuary right for agriculture, forestry, fishing, and animal husbandry, created by the 2010 Civil Code reform to replace the abolished 永佃權. Term-limited to a maximum of 20 years.
notes: Art. 850-1 through 850-9 (added 民國99年). Maximum term 20 years; terms exceeding this are shortened to 20 years (Art. 850-3). Transferable with notice to the landowner (Art. 850-5). Replaces the old 永佃權 which was abolished in the same reform. No precise common-law or German equivalent.

description: A sectional superficies right over a specific stratum above or below the land surface, rather than the entire vertical column. Critical for modern urban development (underground infrastructure, elevated rail, subsurface utilities).
notes: TW: Art. 841-1 through 841-6 (added 民國99年). JP: Art. 269-2 (区分地上権). Allows creation of superficies rights limited to a defined stratum, enabling layered development. The ordinary 地上權 covers the full vertical column; 區分地上權 partitions it. No direct common-law equivalent, though strata title and airspace rights serve partially analogous functions.
